extra.reasoning:
The property requires globally(opted_out implies not sent), i.e., no reachable state has opted_out=true and sent=true. The only transitions setting sent:=true are t2 (send_attempt if not opted_out — safe, since opted_out is false there) and t5 (send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=6). Transition t5 directly produces opted_out=true with sent=true, which violates the property, provided a state with opted_out=true and ticks>=6 is reachable.

Such a state is reachable: fire optout (t1: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0), then six tick events (t4 increments ticks 0->1->2->3->4->5->6, guard is true and fires regardless of opted_out; sent stays false). After the sixth tick, opted_out=true, ticks=6, sent=false. Now a send_attempt enables t5 (opted_out and 6>=6), which sets sent:=true. Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true — violating opted_out implies not sent.

This 8-event execution is within the 10-event bound. Note that on the final send_attempt both t3 (sets sent=false) and t5 (sets sent=true) are enabled (nondeterminism), but since the property must hold on every reachable execution, the t5 branch is a genuine reachable violation. Transition t5 is the bug: it re-enables sending after opt-out once enough ticks have elapsed, contradicting the plain-language intent that an opted-out recipient is never sent to.
